- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# Deep Cove Research
2	
3	High-integrity research pipeline: turns a plain-language research
4	question into a grounded, per-sentence-verified markdown report with
5	bibliography, corpus-approval gate, and a separate-family evaluator.
6	
7	> **Internal codename:** *Polaris*. You will still see `polaris_*` packages, `PG_*` env flags, and
8	> `/workspace/POLARIS` paths throughout the code — that is the engine's codename, not the product
9	> name. De-branding of internal identifiers is deliberate and gradual so it never destabilizes the
10	> running pipeline.
11	
12	## v6.2 mission state (2026-05-04)
13	
14	POLARIS is shipping toward a **Sep 6, 2026 tracer demo for incoming PM
15	Carney**, governed by `polaris-controls/PLAN.md` (admin-only, signed).
16	The demo target is end-to-end clinical research: scope discovery →
17	ambiguity detection → tiered retrieval → generator with strict-verify →
18	GPG-signed audit bundle → BEAT-BOTH benchmark vs ChatGPT-DR / Gemini-DR.
19	
20	| Slice | Deliverable | Substrate |
21	|---|---|---|
22	| 1 | Scope discovery + ambiguity | `polaris_graph/scope/`, `/api/intake`, `/intake` page |
23	| 2 | Tiered retrieval (Serper + S2) | `polaris_graph/clinical_retrieval/`, `/api/retrieval`, `/retrieval` page |
24	| 3 | Generator + strict-verify | `polaris_graph/clinical_generator/`, `/api/generation`, `/generation` page |
25	| 4 | Audit bundle GPG-signed | `polaris_graph/audit_bundle/`, `/api/audit-bundle`, download in `/generation` |
26	| 5 | BEAT-BOTH benchmark + demo polish | `polaris_graph/benchmark/`, `/api/benchmark`, `/benchmark` page, `scripts/run_benchmark.py` |
27	
28	Operator entry points:
29	
30	- **Demo runbook:** `docs/demo_runbook.md` (env setup → boot → walkthrough)
31	- **Mission status:** `docs/mission_status.md` (single-page state of play)
32	- **Pre-demo smoke:** `PYTHONPATH=src python scripts/demo_smoke.py -v`
33	- **Seed benchmark UI:** `python scripts/seed_demo_benchmark.py --output outputs/demo_benchmark/clinical_n10_demo`
34	- **Home walkthrough:** `http://localhost:3000/` four-step click-through
35	
36	## Heritage pipelines (pre-v6.2)
37	
38	POLARIS also hosts three heritage pipelines from prior cycles. These
39	are the V30 honest-rebuild engine (305 tests, 159 commits in the 60
40	days before the v6.2 cutover) plus the legacy CLI. See
41	`architecture.md` and `docs/live_code_audit.md` for the full history.
42	
43	| Pipeline | Purpose | Entry | Status |
44	|---|---|---|---|
45	| **A. Honest-rebuild** | Clean-room sweep orchestrator. Writes per-query manifests + `report.md` under `outputs/honest_sweep_*/`. Used for the 8-query validation sweep. | `python -m scripts.run_honest_sweep_r3` | Active — 159 commits in last 60 days. Hardened via 5-round Codex↔Claude audit (see `outputs/codex_findings/`). |
46	| **B. UI server** | FastAPI web server that the Docker default entrypoint (`serve`) launches. Hosts a single-vector research UI that uses the v1/v2/v3 LangGraph variants under `src/polaris_graph/`. | `uvicorn scripts.live_server:app` (Docker default) | Active — last updated 2026-04-17. |
47	| **C. Legacy CLI research** | Docker `research` subcommand → `full_cycle.py` → `src/orchestration/graph.run_research()`. | `python -m scripts.full_cycle` | **Frozen since 2026-03-16**. Also has broken imports (`scripts/final_audit.py` and `scripts/run_ragas_v3.py` no longer exist). See `src/orchestration/FROZEN_SINCE_2026-03-16.md`. |
48	
49	> The prior README advertised a fictional 13-phase "P0-P12" pipeline
50	> that has not existed in this repo for months. That document was
51	> archived on 2026-04-18 and replaced with the current-state description
52	> in `architecture.md`.
53	
54	## Quick start — pipeline A (the one we actively test)
55	
56	```bash
57	# 1. Install
58	python -m venv .venv
59	.venv\Scripts\activate    # Windows
60	# source .venv/bin/activate  # macOS/Linux
61	pip install -r requirements.txt
62	
63	# 2. Configure — set these in .env
64	#    OPENROUTER_API_KEY=...     (required for generator + evaluator)
65	#    SERPER_API_KEY=...         (required for primary web search)
66	#    SEMANTIC_SCHOLAR_API_KEY=... (optional — academic search)
67	
68	# 3. Run one query against the 8-query validation sweep
69	python -m scripts.run_honest_sweep_r3 \

1	"""Demonstrate the oracle as a working REGRESSION GATE on a produced artifact.
2	
3	With the LLM I/O frozen by the cassette, the ONLY thing that can change the output artifact is the
4	code. So:
5	  * an unchanged 'refactor' replays a **byte-identical** artifact  -> PASS,
6	  * a real behaviour change produces a **different** artifact       -> the byte-diff CATCHES it.
7	
8	This is the "byte-compare final observable outputs across a frozen trace" property codex flagged as
9	the requirement for using the oracle to verify refactors.
10	
11	    /home/polaris/pipeline-env/bin/python tests/oracle/demo_artifact_diff.py   (from repo root, PYTHONPATH=.)
12	"""
13	
14	from __future__ import annotations
15	
16	import asyncio
17	
18	from dotenv import load_dotenv
19	
20	load_dotenv("/workspace/POLARIS/.env", override=True)
21	
22	from src.polaris_graph.llm.openrouter_client import OpenRouterClient  # noqa: E402
23	from tests.oracle.llm_cassette import llm_cassette  # noqa: E402
24	
25	TAPE = "tests/oracle/cassettes/demo_artifact.jsonl"
26	
27	
28	async def assemble(joiner) -> str:
29	    """A tiny 'pipeline unit': two LLM calls assembled into a formatted artifact."""
30	    client = OpenRouterClient(model="z-ai/glm-5.2")
31	    a = (await client.generate("Reply with exactly one word: apple", max_tokens=8, temperature=0.0)).content
32	    b = (await client.generate("Reply with exactly one word: banana", max_tokens=8, temperature=0.0)).content
33	    return joiner(a, b)
34	
35	
36	def join_v1(a: str, b: str) -> str:
37	    return f"# Report\n- {a}\n- {b}\n"
38	
39	
40	def join_v2(a: str, b: str) -> str:
41	    # a 'refactor' that actually changes observable behaviour (bullets -> numbers)
42	    return f"# Report\n1. {a}\n2. {b}\n"
43	
44	
45	def main() -> None:
46	    with llm_cassette(TAPE, "record"):                       # 1 paid run
47	        golden = asyncio.run(assemble(join_v1))
48	    print("GOLDEN artifact recorded:\n" + golden)
49	
50	    with llm_cassette(TAPE, "replay"):                       # unchanged code, frozen I/O
51	        same = asyncio.run(assemble(join_v1))
52	    assert same == golden, "unchanged refactor must replay byte-identical"
53	    print("PASS: unchanged code replays byte-identical (no false regression).")
54	
55	    with llm_cassette(TAPE, "replay"):                       # a real behaviour change
56	        changed = asyncio.run(assemble(join_v2))
57	    assert changed != golden, "a behaviour change must produce a different artifact"
58	    print("CAUGHT: the behaviour change produced a different artifact — regression detected:")
59	    print("  golden :", repr(golden))
60	    print("  changed:", repr(changed))
61	    print("=== ORACLE IS A WORKING REGRESSION GATE: byte-identical on no-op, diff on real change ===")
62	
63	
64	if __name__ == "__main__":
65	    main()

input.content:
# Reproducible build & supply-chain provenance

Status: **S6 (review-readiness) — supply-chain hardening.**
Scope of this document: **build / dependency / documentation artifacts only.** No `src/`
runtime logic is involved in anything described here.

This document is the single source of truth for how to build POLARIS from a pinned,
hash-verified dependency set, how to (re)generate the supply-chain artifacts, and how to
prove two independent builds produce byte-identical results.

---

## 1. Pinned Python version

The runtime is pinned to a single interpreter patch version:

| Pin | File | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Interpreter floor/exact | `.python-version` | `3.11.10` |

`.python-version` is honoured by `pyenv` and by `uv` when it selects/provisions an
interpreter, so a fresh checkout resolves to the same interpreter used to compile the
lock and the SBOM.

**Why `.python-version` and not `requires-python` in `pyproject.toml`:** `pyproject.toml`
in this repo is **tooling-only** (it carries `[tool.ruff]`, `[tool.mypy]`,
`[tool.interrogate]` and intentionally has **no `[project]` and no `[build-system]`
table**). The project is run from source; it is **not** a pip-installable/buildable
package. Adding a `[project]` table purely to hold `requires-python` would falsely signal
that the repo is a distributable wheel and would change tooling assumptions. The
interpreter contract is therefore expressed with `.python-version`, which matches how the
lock was originally compiled (`uv pip compile --python-version 3.11 ...`).

---

## 2. Dependency locking & cryptographic hashes

### 2.1 Files

| File | Pinned (`==`) | Cryptographic hashes | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| `requirements.txt` | partial | no | Hand-maintained convenience set. **Not** for reproducible installs (self-described as non-minimal). |
| `requirements.lock` | yes (287 pkgs) | **no** | Original uv-compiled version lock. Kept for provenance/diffing. |
| `requirements.hashed.txt` | yes (287 pkgs) | **yes (5125 `--hash=sha256:` lines)** | **The reproducible-install source of truth.** |

`requirements.hashed.txt` was **generated, not hand-written.** It was produced from the
existing pinned `requirements.lock` with `--generate-hashes`, so every one of the 287
pinned versions is carried through unchanged and each is now bound to its full set of
PyPI artifact SHA-256 hashes. Version drift between `requirements.lock` and
`requirements.hashed.txt` is **zero** (verified by diffing the `name==version` pin sets).

### 2.2 Regenerating the hashed lock

The lock is regenerated with [`uv`](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/). `uv` is the tool that
produced the original lock (see the header comment in `requirements.lock`).

```bash
# From the repo root, with uv >= 0.11 available.
uv pip compile \
    --python-version 3.11 \
    --generate-hashes \
    requirements.lock \
    -o requirements.hashed.txt
```

Compiling **from `requirements.lock`** (rather than re-resolving from the original
loose inputs `req-stripped.txt` + `requirements-v6.txt`, one of which no longer exists in
the tree) guarantees the pinned versions cannot move — the step only *adds hashes* to an
already-frozen version set.

> Tooling note for this environment: `uv`, `pip-tools`, `cyclonedx-py`, and `pip-audit`
> are **not installed** in `/home/polaris/pipeline-env` (that env has pip 24.2 + Python
> 3.11.10). The artifacts committed here were generated by installing `uv` and
> `cyclonedx-bom` into a throwaway, isolated location (leaving `pipeline-env` untouched)
> and running the commands above/below. To reproduce on a clean machine:
>
> ```bash
> pip install 'uv>=0.11' 'cyclonedx-bom>=7'
> ```
>
> If `uv` is genuinely unavailable and only `pip-tools` is present, the equivalent is:
>
> ```bash
> pip-compile --generate-hashes --output-file requirements.hashed.txt requirements.lock
> ```
>
> Do **not** hand-edit hashes — they must always come from a resolver.

### 2.3 Reproducible install (hash-enforced)

```bash
# 1. Provision the pinned interpreter (pyenv/uv both honour .python-version).
python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate   # Python 3.11.10

# 2. Install ONLY from the hashed lock, with hash verification MANDATORY.
pip install --require-hashes --no-deps -r requirements.hashed.txt
```

`--require-hashes` makes pip **refuse** any package whose downloaded artifact does not
match a hash in the file, and refuse the whole file if any requirement lacks a hash. This
is the property that turns a version pin into a tamper-evident supply-chain control.
`--no-deps` is safe because the hashed lock is already a fully-resolved, transitively
complete set.

---

## 3. Software Bill of Materials (SBOM)

| File | Format | Components | Reproducible |
|---|---|---|---|
| `sbom.cdx.json` | CycloneDX 1.5 (JSON) | 287 | yes — byte-identical across runs |

`sbom.cdx.json` was **generated** from `requirements.hashed.txt` (the pinned+hashed
source of truth), not from the possibly-drifted live environment. Every component carries
a `pkg:pypi/...` PackageURL and its artifact hashes.

### 3.1 Regenerating the SBOM

```bash
# From the repo root, with cyclonedx-bom (cyclonedx-py) installed.
python -m cyclonedx_py requirements \
    --output-reproducible \
    --sv 1.5 \
    --of JSON \
    -o sbom.cdx.json \
    requirements.hashed.txt
```

`--output-reproducible` strips the non-deterministic fields (random `serialNumber`,
wall-clock timestamps), which is what lets two runs be byte-identical — verified here:
regenerating `sbom.cdx.json` a second time produced a `diff`-clean, byte-identical file.

An equivalent SBOM can be exported directly by `uv`:

```bash
uv export --format cyclonedx1.5 --no-emit-project -o sbom.cdx.json
```

(Prefer the `cyclonedx_py requirements` form above so the SBOM is bound to exactly the
committed `requirements.hashed.txt` rather than to a `uv.lock`, which this repo does not
carry.)

### 3.2 Vulnerability audit (documented step)

`pip-audit` is not installed in `pipeline-env`. To audit the pinned set against advisory
databases:

```bash
pip install pip-audit
pip-audit -r requirements.hashed.txt            # audit the lock directly
# or, against the CycloneDX SBOM:
pip-audit --format cyclonedx-json --input sbom.cdx.json
```

This is a **documented manual step**, not an artifact committed by S6.

---

## 4. Two-build hash-match verification

The goal: prove the build is reproducible by building it **twice in independent clean
environments**, hashing the produced artifacts, and diffing. Any difference is a
non-determinism bug to be chased down.

This mirrors the **deterministic-oracle byte-diff** precedent already in the repo
(`tests/oracle/demo_artifact_diff.py`), which freezes the LLM I/O with a cassette so the
only thing that can change an output artifact is the code, then asserts a **byte-identical**
replay on a no-op and a **diff** on a real change. Here we apply the same
"byte-compare final observable outputs" discipline to *build* artifacts instead of
pipeline outputs.

### 4.1 Method

```bash
# --- Build A: clean env #1 ---
git clean -xdf && python -m venv .venvA && source .venvA/bin/activate
pip install --require-hashes --no-deps -r requirements.hashed.txt
python -m cyclonedx_py requirements --output-reproducible --sv 1.5 --of JSON \
    -o /tmp/sbomA.cdx.json requirements.hashed.txt
sha256sum requirements.hashed.txt /tmp/sbomA.cdx.json > /tmp/hashesA.txt
deactivate

# --- Build B: clean env #2 (fresh checkout / fresh venv) ---
git clean -xdf && python -m venv .venvB && source .venvB/bin/activate
pip install --require-hashes --no-deps -r requirements.hashed.txt
python -m cyclonedx_py requirements --output-reproducible --sv 1.5 --of JSON \
    -o /tmp/sbomB.cdx.json requirements.hashed.txt
sha256sum requirements.hashed.txt /tmp/sbomB.cdx.json > /tmp/hashesB.txt
deactivate

# --- Compare: must be byte-identical ---
diff /tmp/sbomA.cdx.json /tmp/sbomB.cdx.json && echo "SBOM: byte-identical"
diff <(cut -d' ' -f1 /tmp/hashesA.txt) <(cut -d' ' -f1 /tmp/hashesB.txt) \
    && echo "HASH-MATCH: two builds agree"
```

- The **dependency layer** is verified reproducible by `--require-hashes`: both builds
  install exactly the same artifacts (same SHA-256s) or fail.
- The **SBOM artifact** is verified reproducible by the `diff` on `sbomA` vs `sbomB`;
  `--output-reproducible` is what makes this hold. This was confirmed in-place for S6:
  regenerating the SBOM twice yielded a byte-identical file.

### 4.2 Recorded S6 artifact digests

Reference digests for the artifacts committed in S6 (recompute with `sha256sum` to
verify a checkout matches):

```
requirements.hashed.txt : eff7c152c721eeeea043b568e7b92310bbdeb278db61c6af7f7f3b7fc7b69a69
sbom.cdx.json           : fdfbc55f454f04fa4b6f94fdcc595bd29c32b2cf9a5ca2d4aa10abd48c0a86ce
```

> If you regenerate these files with a newer `uv`/`cyclonedx-bom` release, the digests
> above may legitimately change (tool version, hash-set completeness). The invariant that
> must always hold is **two builds on the same tool versions agree byte-for-byte**, and
> **the pinned `name==version` set never drifts from `requirements.lock`.**

---

## 5. What is automated vs a documented manual step (honesty ledger)

| Item | S6 status |
|---|---|
| `.python-version` pin (`3.11.10`) | **Committed artifact.** |
| `requirements.hashed.txt` (287 pkgs, 5125 real SHA-256 hashes, zero version drift) | **Generated & committed** (real, from `uv`). |
| `sbom.cdx.json` (CycloneDX 1.5, 287 components, reproducible) | **Generated & committed** (real, from `cyclonedx_py`). |
| Hashed-lock regeneration command | **Documented** (§2.2) — re-runnable. |
| SBOM regeneration command | **Documented** (§3.1) — re-runnable, verified byte-identical. |
| `--require-hashes` install command | **Documented** (§2.3). |
| Two-build hash-match procedure | **Documented** (§4) — SBOM leg verified in-place. |
| `pip-audit` vulnerability scan | **Documented manual step** (§3.2) — tool not installed in `pipeline-env`. |

`uv`, `cyclonedx-bom`, and `pip-audit` are **not** part of `pipeline-env`; the two
committed artifacts were produced by installing `uv` + `cyclonedx-bom` into an isolated
throwaway location so the runtime environment was never mutated. No hashes or SBOM
components were ever hand-written — everything committed came out of a resolver/generator.

---

## 6. Build-doc pointer fix

The README "Quick start" installs the hand-maintained, un-hashed `requirements.txt`. For
a **reproducible, supply-chain-verified** install, use the hashed lock instead:

```bash
python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate      # Python 3.11.10 (see .python-version)
pip install --require-hashes --no-deps -r requirements.hashed.txt
```

`requirements.txt` remains as a developer convenience; `requirements.hashed.txt` is the
build-of-record.
